items.q: If a machine gives a mechanical advantage of 5, do you get force for nothing?
items.correct: No — the force is multiplied by 5 but the effort must move roughly five times further than the load; work output never exceeds work input, and friction wastes some.
items.misconception: Machines multiply force without any trade-off, giving free extra work.
items.random_wrong: Mechanical advantage means the machine uses less electricity.
items.why: Students focus on the force gain and forget the distance trade-off; conservation of energy means mechanisms trade distance for force, never create work.

items.q: Does a larger driven gear make the output spin faster?
items.correct: No — a driven gear larger than the driver turns more slowly, by the ratio of the teeth, but delivers more torque; a smaller driven gear spins faster with less torque.
items.misconception: Bigger gears always rotate faster because they are more powerful.
items.random_wrong: Gear size only affects how loudly the gearbox runs.
items.why: Students link size with speed; the inverse speed-torque relationship set by tooth ratio is the core idea behind every gear calculation.

items.q: In which class of lever is the load between the fulcrum and the effort?
items.correct: A second-class lever, such as a wheelbarrow; first-class levers have the fulcrum in the middle, and third-class levers have the effort in the middle.
items.misconception: A wheelbarrow is a first-class lever because it is the most common example.
items.random_wrong: Lever classes are decided by the material the lever is made from.
items.why: Students rank classes by familiarity instead of arrangement; identifying which element sits in the middle is the reliable way to classify any lever.

items.q: A 20-tooth driver gear meshes with a 60-tooth driven gear. What happens to the output speed?
items.correct: It is reduced to one third of the input speed
items.why: It's It is reduced to one third of the input speed. “It triples” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A wheelbarrow, with the load between the wheel and the handles, is which class of lever?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Second class. “First class” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A crank and slider converts which types of motion?
items.why: It's Rotary to reciprocating motion. “Linear to oscillating motion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Two meshed spur gears rotate in which directions?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Opposite directions. “The same direction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A lever moves a 300 N load with an effort of 60 N. What is its mechanical advantage?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 ratio. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
